titleEn: Beyond Disruption: Earning Trust and Driving Impact in AgTech Through Grower-Led Innovation
titleZh: 超越颠覆：通过种植者主导的创新在农业科技中赢得信任与推动影响
summaryZh: 本文由Reservoir CEO Danny Bernstein撰写，反思硅谷式快速迭代文化在农业领域的局限性，提出“物理人工智能”概念——即技术必须深入田间地头，与作物和土壤实时互动。文章强调农业科技不应孤立开发，而应让初创企业、种植者和零售商在类似“奥运村”的创新社区中协同工作，在真实农场环境中测试技术。虽然主要聚焦种植业，但其中关于技术落地、信任建立和实地验证的理念对智慧畜牧行业同样具有借鉴意义。
featuredReason: 本文提出的“物理AI”和“种植者主导创新”理念，对智慧畜牧行业在技术落地、信任建立和实地验证方面具有重要借鉴意义，值得畜牧科技从业者反思如何让技术真正服务于养殖一线。
contentFull: By CropLife Share Innovation in agriculture has taken big steps forward — with drones mapping fields, satellites delivering real-time data, and software guiding decisions at scale, writes Reservoir CEO Danny Bernstein at CropLife. But there’s a layer of complexity closer to the ground for open-air farms — rugged terrain, fragile crops, unpredictable labor — that demands something more tactile. That’s where physical artificial intelligence (AI) comes in: technology built to operate in the dirt, alongside the crop, in real time. Silicon Valley software culture shaped my career, where things move fast, break often, and scale is king. At Google and Microsoft, we obsessed over clean UX, seamless code, and shipping quickly. But agriculture plays by a different clock. The unpredictability of open air and fields doesn’t care about your roadmap or your next release. And the stakes — grower balance sheets, rural economics, ag communities, food systems — are deeply real. That said, there’s something from Silicon Valley tech that feels worth bringing with us — not the hype, but the habit of questioning assumptions. What if startups worked in sync with growers from the start? What if we built tools that adapt to the timing of the crop, instead of forcing the crop to fit our schedule? What if the people designing the tech were in the field when it gets used? That’s the thinking behind the Reservoir — an “Olympic Village” innovation community for agtech, starting in California, where startups, growers, and retailers work side-by-side. Ideas get pressure-tested in the field, not in isolation. MORE BY CROPLIFE Digital Farming June 18, 2026 9 Ways Collaboration Is Accelerating Innovation Across AgTech By CropLife|June 18, 2026 Data Management June 12, 2026 Tech Hub LIVE Returns to Des Moines July 20-22 with Practical Answers for a High-Pressure Ag Economy By CropLife|June 12, 2026 Analytics March 8, 2026 EarthDaily’s Dave Gebhardt on the Future of Satellite Data in Agriculture By CropLife|March 8, 2026 From Disruption to Ground-Level Results Too often, agtech is built in isolation — by teams removed from the realities of the farm, designing for ideal conditions that don’t exist. But agriculture isn’t always a controlled environment or a software layer you can iterate on overnight. It’s shaped by unpredictable variables — weather, labor, regulation, crop biology — that don’t respond to roadmaps or pitch decks. Top Articles Read More Read More Read More Read More Read More Syngenta Signs MoU to Be Strategic Partner in India’s First Open-Data Agricultural Ecosystem Annam.AI What we keep hearing from growers, retailers and producers is that real traction happens when technologists are hands-on, immersed in the day-to-day of the farm, not just the boardroom. And that’s backed by data. According to the 2024 CropLife/Purdue University Precision Agriculture Dealership Survey, the percentage of ag retailers using drones to apply crop inputs is projected to grow from 35% today to more than 50% by 2027. AI-driven weed identification, once cutting-edge, is on track to be a standard offering for a quarter of dealers in the same window. Yet for all the progress, the reality is sobering: less than 2% of specialty crop production is currently automated, according to the Western Growers Association. That’s a massive gap — and a massive opportunity. Read more at CropLife. 0 1 5 Beyond Disruption: Earning Trust and Driving Impact in AgTech Through Grower-Led Innovation Subscribe Today For Subscribe CropLife, a sister brand to the Global Ag Tech Initiative at Meister Media Worldwide, is the nation’s brand leader in ag retail communication. See all author stories here. Topics: featured-home
translationZh:
超越颠覆：通过种植者主导的创新在农业科技中赢得信任与推动影响
作者：CropLife

农业创新已经迈出了大步——无人机测绘农田、卫星提供实时数据、软件大规模指导决策，Reservoir CEO Danny Bernstein在CropLife上写道。但对于露天农场来说，地面附近存在一层更复杂的因素——崎岖的地形、脆弱的作物、不可预测的劳动力——这需要更触手可及的东西。这就是物理人工智能（AI）的用武之地：一种旨在泥土中、在作物旁边实时运行的技术。

硅谷的软件文化塑造了我的职业生涯，在那里，事情进展快、经常出问题、规模至上。在谷歌和微软，我们痴迷于干净的UX、无缝的代码和快速交付。但农业遵循着不同的节奏。露天田野的不可预测性不会在意你的路线图或下一次发布。而且，赌注——种植者的资产负债表、农村经济、农业社区、食品系统——是真实而深远的。

尽管如此，硅谷技术中有些东西值得随身携带——不是炒作，而是质疑假设的习惯。如果初创企业从一开始就与种植者协同工作会怎样？如果我们构建的工具能适应作物的生长节奏，而不是强迫作物适应我们的时间表会怎样？如果设计技术的人在技术被使用时就在田间会怎样？这就是Reservoir背后的理念——一个位于加利福尼亚的农业科技“奥运村”创新社区，让初创企业、种植者和零售商并肩工作。想法在田间经受压力测试，而不是在孤立环境中。

更多来自CropLife的数字农业文章：2026年6月18日《协作加速农业科技创新的9种方式》；2026年6月12日《Tech Hub LIVE重返得梅因，为高压农业经济提供实用答案》；2026年3月8日《EarthDaily的Dave Gebhardt谈卫星数据在农业中的未来》。

从颠覆到地面成果

农业科技常常是在孤立环境中构建的——由远离农场现实的团队设计，为不存在的理想条件而设计。但农业并不总是一个受控环境或一个可以一夜迭代的软件层。它由不可预测的变量塑造——天气、劳动力、法规、作物生物学——这些变量不会响应路线图或推销演示。

热门文章：先正达签署谅解备忘录，成为印度首个开放数据农业生态系统的战略合作伙伴；Annam.AI。

我们从种植者、零售商和生产者那里不断听到的是，真正的进展发生在技术人员亲力亲为、沉浸在农场的日常运作中，而不仅仅是董事会会议室里。而且，这有数据支持。根据2024年CropLife/普渡大学精准农业经销商调查，使用无人机施用作物投入品的农业零售商比例预计将从35%增长到……
author: Matt Hopkins
contentHtml:
Smarter agtech starts with trust — discover how grower-led innovation is driving real impact beyond disruption, says Reservoir CEO Danny Bernstein.
The post Beyond Disruption: Earning Trust and Driving Impact in AgTech Through Grower-Led Innovation appeared first on Global Ag Tech Initiative.
